figures.caption: Figure 16: Extended Data Fig. 9: Reproducibility across seeds. Per-seed values of b, DRM lure FA, and deff for Vector DB and Graph architectures. Low variance confirms reproducibility. n = 5 seeds.

Figure 17: Extended Data Fig. 10: Full solution analysis. a, b vs. nominal dimensionality. b, BM25 immunity vs. usefulness. c, Orthogonalisation methods. d, Compression: b (blue) and accuracy (red) vs. cluster count. Every solution traces a tradeoff; none achieves both immunity and usefulness.
Table 5: Solution analysis data points.

Figure 8: Extended Data Fig. 1: The five memory architectures. Each architecture implements a fundamentally different storage and retrieval mechanism: cosine similarity (Vector DB), attention over context (Attention), BM25 + LLM re-ranking (Filesystem), personalised PageRank (Graph), and parametric knowledge in weights (Parametric). Despite architectural diversity, all except Filesystem strongly satisfy SPP (p < 0.001). n = 143 sentence pairs per architecture.

Figure 9: Extended Data Fig. 2: Vector Database full results. a, Forgetting exponent b at each competitor count, showing monotonic increase. b, DRM hit rate, lure FA, and unrelated FA. c, Spacing retention: long = 0.902, massed = 0.360. d, Eigenvalue spectrum (deff = 158). Error bars: bootstrap 95% CI, n = 5 seeds.

Figure 5: Effective dimensionality converges far below nominal regardless of architecture. deff (participation ratio) vs. dnom for all five architectures. Grey: biological range (deff = 100–5006,7). Qwen hidden states (dnom = 3,584) compress to deff = 17.9, a 200× reduction. All architectures cluster below the interference threshold.

Figure 6: No proposed solution achieves both immunity and usefulness. Every solution that reduces interference moves along a tradeoff frontier toward reduced usefulness; no solution escapes the frontier itself. This is the empirical corollary to Theorem 1. a, Zero-padding does not reduce b (deff unchanged). b, BM25 eliminates false recall but semantic agreement drops to 15.5%. c, Gram–Schmidt eliminates interference; semantic accuracy = 0%. d, Compression reduces b but degrades retrieval.

Figure 12: Extended Data Fig. 5: Parametric Memory full results. PopQA interference: accuracy drops from 1.000 (< 50 neighbours) to 0.113 (> 1,000 neighbours). Power-law fit b = 0.215. DRM FA = 0 behaviourally. TOT = 69%, a very high partial retrieval rate. deff = 17.9. n = 3 seeds for PopQA.

Figure 11: Extended Data Fig. 4: Attention Memory full results. a, Phase transition: near-perfect accuracy at nnear < 100, then catastrophic collapse (logistic fit: n0 ≈ 120, k ≈ 0.03; power-law fitting is inappropriate for this sigmoid failure mode; y-axis values reflect interference severity, not power-law exponents). b, DRM FA = 0 at behavioural level (geometric prediction holds: 24/24 lures within caps). c, Spacing: architectural capacity artefact: massed = 1.0, spaced = 0.0 (context-window limit relocates interference to capacity domain). d, deff = 17.9 from dnom = 3,584, a 200× compression. n = 5 seeds.

Figure 10: Extended Data Fig. 3: Graph Memory full results. a, b = 0.478 at 10,000 competitors. b, DRM lure FA = 0.208 at θ = 0.82. c, Spacing: long = 0.996, massed = 0.920. d, Eigenvalue spectrum (deff = 127). n = 5 seeds.

Figure 7: Architecture comparison across four memory phenomena. Heatmap of forgetting exponent b, DRM lure FA, spacing Cohen’s d, and TOT rate for all five architectures and human reference. The three prototypical behavioural categories are visible: pure geometric (top two rows), reasoning overlay (middle), SPP-violating (bottom). Dashes indicate metrics not measurable for that architecture (attention b: sigmoid, not power-law; parametric spacing: no controlled paradigm). †Parametric TOT (69%) uses a different operational definition than human/embedding TOT and is not directly comparable (see Discussion). Metrics are architecture-specific and not all directly numerically comparable (see Methods for protocol differences).

Figure 4: False recall is geometrically inevitable. a, Hit rate, lure false alarm rate, and unrelated FA for all five architectures and human data. Embedding architectures show elevated lure FA; LLM architectures show FA = 0 at behavioural level (explicit list-checking). b, Lure FA rates compared directly. The geometric prediction (24/24 lures within spherical caps) holds for all architectures regardless of behavioural output. n = 5 seeds, 24 DRM lists.

Figure 3: The forgetting exponent depends on competitor count, not architecture. Forgetting exponent b vs. number of near competitors for embedding architectures (Vector DB, Graph) with human reference (b ≈ 0.5, dashed). Both converge toward the human range at high competitor counts. Shaded: bootstrap 95% CI, n = 5 seeds.

Figure 15: Extended Data Fig. 8: Spherical cap verification. a, Analytical cap volume (fraction of sphere) vs. dimension for five cap half-angles θ ∈ {10◦, 20◦, 30◦, 45◦, 60◦}, showing exponential collapse with increasing d. Shaded region marks the interference regime (deff ≈ 10–50) where all tested architectures operate. b, Monte Carlo verification: simulated vs. analytical cap volume on log–log axes for the 7 (d, θ) combinations where the Monte Carlo sample detected non-zero signal. Six of seven points fall within ±20% of the y = x line; the single outlier (d = 8, θ = 20◦, ratio = 0.48) reflects finite-sample resolution at analytical volume ≈ 8× 10−5, not analytical error. Marker shape encodes dimension; colour encodes θ (matching a). Confirms Theorem 2.

Figure 14: Extended Data Fig. 7: SPP verification. Mean similarity for related pairs (same article) vs. unrelated pairs (different articles) across all five architectures. All satisfy SPP (p < 0.001), with embedding architectures showing stronger separation. n = 143 pairs.

Figure 13: Extended Data Fig. 6: Filesystem Memory full results. BM25 keyword retrieval: b = 0.000 (no forgetting), FA = 0 (no false recall), all spacing conditions = 1.0. SPP correlation r = 0.210; BM25 weakly satisfies semantic proximity. This architecture demonstrates Solution 2: immunity at the cost of usefulness.
Table 3: Per-architecture results summary (n = 5 seeds unless noted).
Table 1: Hyperparameters for all architectures and experiments.

Figure 1: The No-Escape Theorem: logical structure (paper roadmap). This figure maps the paper’s argument. Under the kernel-threshold theorem class (Axioms A1–A5): the semantic kernel and rate-distortion optimality yield finite semantic effective rank (Theorem 1); local regularity yields positive cap mass (Theorem 2); growing memory yields inevitable forgetting (Theorem 3), with power-law arrival and population heterogeneity producing power-law forgetting curves. Independently, associative δ-convexity yields lure inseparability (Theorem 4). No architecture within this class avoids these consequences without abandoning semantic continuity or adding an external symbolic verifier. Each arrow represents a step derived under stated assumptions and supported by empirical tests across the architectures studied here.

Figure 2: Interference produces forgetting across architecturally distinct memory systems. a, Vector DB and b, Graph show smooth power-law forgetting curves converging toward the human range (b ≈ 0.3–0.7, red dashed). c, Attention shows a phase transition (logistic fit: n0 ≈ 120, k ≈ 0.03; power-law fitting is inappropriate for this sigmoid failure mode). d, Filesystem (BM25) shows b = 0 (no semantic interference). e, Parametric (PopQA) shows monotonic accuracy decline with neighbour density. Category 1 systems degrade continuously; Category 2 systems fail discontinuously. n = 5 seeds throughout.
